papers.title: Phishing Detection System: An Ensemble Approach Using Character-Level CNN and Feature Engineering
papers.abstract: In actuality, phishing attacks remain one of the most prevalent cybersecurity risks in existence today, with malevolent actors constantly changing their strategies to successfully trick users. This paper presents an AI model for a phishing detection system that uses an ensemble approach to combine character-level Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and LightGBM with engineered features. Our system uses a character-level CNN to extract sequential features after extracting 36 lexical, structural, and domain-based features from the URLs. On a test dataset of 19,873 URLs, the ensemble model achieves an accuracy of 99.819 percent, precision of 100 percent, recall of 99.635 percent, and ROC-AUC of 99.947 percent. Through a FastAPI-based service with an intuitive user interface, the suggested system has been utilised to offer real-time detection. In contrast, the results demonstrate that the suggested solution performs better than individual models; LightGBM contributes 40 percent and character-CNN contributes 60 percent to the final prediction. The suggested method maintains extremely low false positive rates while doing a good job of identifying contemporary phishing techniques. Index Terms - Phishing detection, machine learning, deep learning, CNN, ensemble methods, cybersecurity, URL analysis
papers.reason: Problem: The user's research idea is a 'phishing URL detection system,' and the paper's title is 'Phishing Detection System: An Ensemble Approach Using Character-Level CNN and Feature Engineering.' Both aim to detect phishing URLs, addressing the same core problem of identifying malicious URLs. | Method: The paper uses a character-level CNN to extract sequential features from URLs, which aligns with the user's idea. However, instead of combining with WHOIS metadata features through a late-fusion MLP, the paper combines the CNN features with 36 engineered lexical, structural, and domain-based features using LightGBM in an ensemble approach, which is a significant difference in the feature set and fusion mechanism. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on the same specific application area: phishing URL detection. Both use URL strings as the primary data type for feature extraction, and the target system is a detection system for phishing attacks. | Contribution: The paper's core contribution is an AI model for a phishing detection system that uses an ensemble approach combining a character-level CNN and LightGBM with engineered features. This directly matches the user's idea of a phishing URL detection system using a character-level CNN for URL string features and combining them with WHOIS metadata features (which are a type of engineered feature) through a late-fusion MLP (LightGBM serves a similar function in combining features). The paper explicitly states, "Our system uses a character-level CNN to extract sequential features after extracting 36 lexical, structural, and domain-based features from the URLs," and describes an "ensemble approach to combine character-level Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and LightGBM with engineered features."

papers.title: eXpose: A Character-Level Convolutional Neural Network with Embeddings For Detecting Malicious URLs, File Paths and Registry Keys
papers.abstract: For years security machine learning research has promised to obviate the need for signature based detection by automatically learning to detect indicators of attack. Unfortunately, this vision hasn't come to fruition: in fact, developing and maintaining today's security machine learning systems can require engineering resources that are comparable to that of signature-based detection systems, due in part to the need to develop and continuously tune the "features" these machine learning systems look at as attacks evolve. Deep learning, a subfield of machine learning, promises to change this by operating on raw input signals and automating the process of feature design and extraction. In this paper we propose the eXpose neural network, which uses a deep learning approach we have developed to take generic, raw short character strings as input (a common case for security inputs, which include artifacts like potentially malicious URLs, file paths, named pipes, named mutexes, and registry keys), and learns to simultaneously extract features and classify using character-level embeddings and convolutional neural network. In addition to completely automating the feature design and extraction process, eXpose outperforms manual feature extraction based baselines on all of the intrusion detection problems we tested it on, yielding a 5%-10% detection rate gain at 0.1% false positive rate compared to these baselines.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's idea focuses on 'A phishing URL detection system'. The paper's abstract states, 'In this paper we propose the eXpose neural network... to take generic, raw short character strings as input (a common case for security inputs, which include artifacts like potentially malicious URLs...)' and the introduction further clarifies, 'In this paper, we demonstrate eXpose's ability to detect malicious URLs, malicious file paths, and malicious registry keys.' This indicates a very similar problem statement, with the paper addressing malicious URL detection as one of its primary tasks. | Method: Both the user's idea and the paper propose a phishing URL detection system using a character-level CNN to extract features from the URL string. However, the paper's method, eXpose, relies solely on URL input strings and uses a dense neural network for classification, whereas the user's idea specifies combining URL features with WHOIS metadata features through a late-fusion MLP, indicating a different multi-modal approach not present in the paper. | Domain: The user's research idea is a phishing URL detection system. The paper explicitly states its application to 'detect malicious URLs' and dedicates a significant portion of its evaluation and results to this specific problem, as seen in sections like 'Results' and 'Table 1. Numbers of artifacts in our research datasets' which includes 'URLs' as a task. This indicates a direct and specific overlap in the application domain. | Contribution: The paper's primary contribution is a character-level CNN for feature extraction from raw strings (including URLs) and classification, which directly overlaps with the user's idea of using a character-level CNN for URL features. However, the user's idea explicitly includes combining these features with WHOIS metadata through a late-fusion MLP, a component not present in the paper's 'eXpose' model which 'relies solely on URL input strings' and does not mention WHOIS metadata.

papers.title: URLNet: Learning a URL Representation with Deep Learning for Malicious URL Detection
papers.abstract: Malicious URLs host unsolicited content and are used to perpetrate cybercrimes. It is imperative to detect them in a timely manner. Traditionally, this is done through the usage of blacklists, which cannot be exhaustive, and cannot detect newly generated malicious URLs. To address this, recent years have witnessed several efforts to perform Malicious URL Detection using Machine Learning. The most popular and scalable approaches use lexical properties of the URL string by extracting Bag-of-words like features, followed by applying machine learning models such as SVMs. There are also other features designed by experts to improve the prediction performance of the model. These approaches suffer from several limitations: (i) Inability to effectively capture semantic meaning and sequential patterns in URL strings; (ii) Requiring substantial manual feature engineering; and (iii) Inability to handle unseen features and generalize to test data. To address these challenges, we propose URLNet, an end-to-end deep learning framework to learn a nonlinear URL embedding for Malicious URL Detection directly from the URL. Specifically, we apply Convolutional Neural Networks to both characters and words of the URL String to learn the URL embedding in a jointly optimized framework. This approach allows the model to capture several types of semantic information, which was not possible by the existing models. We also propose advanced word-embeddings to solve the problem of too many rare words observed in this task. We conduct extensive experiments on a large-scale dataset and show a significant performance gain over existing methods. We also conduct ablation studies to evaluate the performance of various components of URLNet.
papers.reason: Problem: The paper addresses the problem of 'Malicious URL Detection' and 'Phishing URL Detection' is a specific type of malicious URL detection. The paper's abstract states, 'Malicious URLs host unsolicited content and are used to perpetrate cybercrimes. It is imperative to detect them in a timely manner.' This directly aligns with the user's research idea of a phishing URL detection system, as phishing is a form of cybercrime perpetrated via malicious URLs. | Method: The paper proposes URLNet, which uses character-level CNNs to extract features from URL strings, similar to the user's idea. However, the paper combines these with word-level CNNs in a jointly optimized framework, rather than WHOIS metadata features through a late-fusion MLP as specified in the user's idea. While both use character-level CNNs for URL string feature extraction, the other feature sources and fusion mechanisms differ significantly. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on 'phishing URL detection' and 'Malicious URL Detection' respectively, which are the same specific application area. Both aim to classify URLs as malicious or benign, using the URL string as primary data. The paper explicitly states its goal is to 'classify a given URL as malicious or not' and addresses 'Malicious URL Detection'. | Contribution: The paper 'URLNet' proposes a phishing URL detection system using character-level and word-level CNNs to learn URL embeddings, which aligns with the user's idea of using a character-level CNN for URL string features. However, the user's idea explicitly includes combining these features with WHOIS metadata features through a late-fusion MLP, which is not a claimed contribution of the 'URLNet' paper, as it focuses primarily on lexical features and deep learning for representation learning from the URL string itself, and mentions host-based features (including WHOIS) as 'related work' but not as part of its proposed URLNet architecture or contributions.

papers.title: PhishGuard: A Convolutional Neural Network Based Model for Detecting Phishing URLs with Explainability Analysis
papers.abstract: Cybersecurity is one of the global issues because of the extensive dependence on cyber systems of individuals, industries, and organizations. Among the cyber attacks, phishing is increasing tremendously and affecting the global economy. Therefore, this phenomenon highlights the vital need for enhancing user awareness and robust support at both individual and organizational levels. Phishing URL identification is the best way to address the problem. Various machine learning and deep learning methods have been proposed to automate the detection of phishing URLs. However, these approaches often need more convincing accuracy and rely on datasets consisting of limited samples. Furthermore, these black box intelligent models decision to detect suspicious URLs needs proper explanation to understand the features affecting the output. To address the issues, we propose a 1D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and trained the model with extensive features and a substantial amount of data. The proposed model outperforms existing works by attaining an accuracy of 99.85%. Additionally, our explainability analysis highlights certain features that significantly contribute to identifying the phishing URL.

Both the user's idea and the paper address the exact same core problem of detecting phishing URLs. | Method: The user's idea proposes a character-level CNN for URL features combined with WHOIS metadata via late-fusion MLP. The paper uses a 1D CNN for URL feature extraction, but it extracts 21 'categorical and numerical features' from the URLs rather than character-level features, and it does not mention the use of WHOIS metadata or a late-fusion MLP. While both use CNNs for URL-based phishing detection, the specific feature extraction (character-level vs. engineered features) and the absence of WHOIS metadata and late-fusion MLP in the paper represent meaningful differences. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on the same specific application area: phishing URL detection. Both use URL strings as the primary data type for feature extraction and aim to identify phishing websites, which constitutes the same target system. | Contribution: The paper proposes a 1D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for phishing URL detection, which aligns with the user's idea of using a character-level CNN to extract features from the URL string. However, the paper's methodology focuses on extracting 21 features from URLs using custom functions and regular expressions, which are then fed into the CNN, rather than a character-level CNN directly processing the URL string. Furthermore, the paper does not mention combining these features with WHOIS metadata features through a late-fusion MLP, which is a distinct component of the user's idea.

papers.title: Look Before You Leap: Detecting Phishing Web Pages by Exploiting Raw URL And HTML Characteristics
papers.abstract: Phishing websites distribute unsolicited content and are frequently used to commit email and internet fraud; detecting them before any user information is submitted is critical. Several efforts have been made to detect these phishing websites in recent years. Most existing approaches use hand-crafted lexical and statistical features from a website's textual content to train classification models to detect phishing web pages. However, these phishing detection approaches have a few challenges, including 1) the tediousness of extracting hand-crafted features, which require specialized domain knowledge to determine which features are useful for a particular platform; and 2) the difficulties encountered by models built on hand-crafted features to capture the semantic patterns in words and characters in URL and HTML content. To address these challenges, this paper proposes WebPhish, an end-to-end deep neural network trained using embedded raw URLs and HTML content to detect website phishing attacks. First, the proposed model automatically employs an embedding technique to extract the corresponding characters into homologous dense vectors. Then, the concatenation layer merges the URL and HTML embedding matrices. Following that, Convolutional layers are used to model its semantic dependencies. Extensive experiments were conducted with real-world phishing data, which yielded an accuracy of 98.1\%, showing that WebPhish outperforms baseline detection approaches in identifying phishing pages.
papers.reason: Problem: The paper addresses the problem of 'phishing URL detection' and 'detecting phishing web pages', which is identical to the user's research idea. Both aim to classify URLs as legitimate or phishing. The paper's abstract explicitly states, 'this paper proposes WebPhish, an end-to-end deep neural network trained using embedded raw URLs and HTML content to detect website phishing attacks.' | Method: The user's idea proposes a character-level CNN for URL features combined with WHOIS metadata via a late-fusion MLP. The paper, WebPhish, uses character-level embeddings for URLs and word-level embeddings for HTML content, concatenates them, and then processes them through convolutional layers followed by fully connected layers. While both use character-level CNN for URLs and a fusion approach, the paper's fusion is earlier (concatenation of embeddings) and uses HTML content instead of WHOIS metadata, representing a meaningful difference in design and components. | Domain: The user's research idea is a 'phishing URL detection system'. The paper's title is 'Look Before You Leap: Detecting Phishing Web Pages by Exploiting Raw URL And HTML Characteristics' and its abstract explicitly states it 'proposes WebPhish, an end-to-end deep neural network trained using embedded raw URLs and HTML content to detect website phishing attacks'. Both the user's idea and the paper are focused on the exact same specific application area of phishing detection, using the same data type (URLs and associated web content). | Contribution: The paper's proposed WebPhish model uses a character-level embedding for URLs and word-level embedding for HTML content, which are then concatenated and fed into convolutional layers, followed by fully connected layers for classification (Abstract, Figure 1, The Proposed Model). This closely aligns with the user's idea of a phishing URL detection system using a character-level CNN for URL features and combining them with other features (HTML content in the paper) through a late-fusion MLP (Fully Connected Layer in the paper). The primary difference is the paper's use of HTML content instead of WHOIS metadata.

linked_sections.paper_title: Phishing Detection System: An Ensemble Approach Using Character-Level CNN and Feature Engineering
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: In actuality, phishing attacks remain one of the most prevalent cybersecurity risks in existence today, with malevolent actors constantly changing their strategies to successfully trick users. This paper presents an AI model for a phishing detection system that uses an ensemble approach to combine character-level Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and LightGBM with engineered features.

linked_sections.paper_title: eXpose: A Character-Level Convolutional Neural Network with Embeddings For Detecting Malicious URLs, File Paths and Registry Keys
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: In this paper we propose the eXpose neural network, which uses a deep learning approach we have developed to take generic, raw short character strings as input (a common case for security inputs, which include artifacts like potentially malicious URLs, file paths, named pipes, named mutexes, and registry keys), and learns to simultaneously extract features and classify using character-level embeddings and convolutional neural network. In addition to completely automating the feature design and extraction process, eXpose outperforms manual feature extraction based baselines on all of the intrusion detection problems we tested it on, yielding a 5%-10% detection rate gain at 0.1% false positive rate compared to these baselines.
linked_sections.reason: The paper's contribution includes using a character-level CNN for malicious URL detection and feature extraction. The user's idea proposes the same core method for feature extraction from URLs. However, the user's idea also includes combining with WHOIS metadata, which is a distinct aspect not fully covered by the paper's primary contribution of relying 'solely on URL input strings'.

layer2_full.summary: Your research idea shows very low originality, with significant overlap in problem, domain, and contribution with existing phishing detection systems. To improve originality, focus on differentiating your core methodology, as this shows the most potential for novelty.
